questions.q: What is a tree-walking interpreter, and what is its primary limitation?
options: It directly executes an AST by recursively visiting nodes; limited by slow dispatch and repeated tree traversals
A tree-walking interpreter is the fastest way to run code because it bypasses compilation
Tree-walking interpreters cannot handle function calls or recursion
Tree-walking is only used for very simple languages without control flow
questions.explanation: A tree-walking interpreter recursively visits AST nodes to execute code. Python and Ruby originally used this. It's straightforward but inefficient—repeated traversals of the same nodes and indirect function dispatch slow execution. JIT compilation and bytecode compilation are faster alternatives.

questions.q: How does bytecode compilation improve upon tree-walking interpretation?
options: Bytecode reduces code to a sequence of low-level instructions, enabling caching and optimized dispatch
Bytecode is just another name for the same tree-walking process
Bytecode compilation is slower than tree-walking because it requires an extra pass
Bytecode can only represent programs with simple expressions
questions.explanation: Bytecode (used in Java, Python) is an intermediate representation between source and machine code. A bytecode interpreter runs a compact instruction sequence via a switch-based dispatch loop, faster than recursing through an AST. Caching bytecode avoids reparsing; JIT compilation can further accelerate it.

questions.q: What is the difference between a stack-based virtual machine and a register-based virtual machine?
options: Stack VMs push/pop values from a stack; register VMs store values in named registers, requiring explicit instructions
Stack-based VMs are always faster than register-based VMs
Register VMs cannot handle function calls or local variables
Both stack and register VMs are equivalent and interchangeable
questions.explanation: Stack VMs (JVM, CPython) use PUSH/POP for operands; register VMs (Lua, Android's ART) use named registers. Stack VMs have shorter instruction streams but slower memory access. Register VMs require more instructions but fewer memory ops. Modern VMs hybrid-approach. Neither is universally faster.

questions.q: What is 'direct threaded code', and how does it speed up bytecode interpretation?
options: Direct threading stores code addresses in bytecode stream, skipping decoding and switch dispatch on each iteration
Direct threading requires recompiling the interpreter for every program you run
Direct threading only works for languages without variables or functions
Direct threading and indirect threading are performance-equivalent
questions.explanation: Bytecode interpreters normally decode opcode → case in switch → execute. Threaded interpreters replace opcodes with direct code pointers, eliminating switch overhead per instruction. Forth and some Lisp systems use direct threading. Drawback: less flexible for bytecode transformation or profiling.

questions.q: What is just-in-time (JIT) compilation, and when is it beneficial?
options: JIT compiles bytecode to native machine code at runtime based on execution patterns; beneficial for frequently executed code
JIT compilation occurs at compile time, before the program runs
JIT is only useful for code that runs once; repeated execution does not benefit
JIT can make programs slower because of compilation overhead
questions.explanation: JIT (HotSpot in Java, PyPy) monitors code execution. Hot paths are compiled to native machine code, drastically speeding them up. Initial runs are slower (compilation cost), but long-running or repeatedly-called functions become faster than bytecode. Profiling informs optimization decisions.

questions.q: What is the 'dispatch problem' in bytecode interpreters, and how can it be solved?
options: Dispatch refers to instruction decoding and branching to the handler; indirect dispatch (switch), direct threading, and JIT compilation all reduce overhead
The dispatch problem is specific to stack-based VMs and does not affect register-based ones
Dispatch overhead is unavoidable and represents a fundamental limit of bytecode interpretation
Eliminating dispatch requires converting to assembly language
questions.explanation: Bytecode interpreters decode opcodes and dispatch to handlers. A switch statement (indirect dispatch) repeats this per instruction. Direct threading (code pointers) and prediction tables speed dispatch. JIT eliminates it by compiling to native code. Low dispatch overhead is critical to interpreter performance.

questions.q: What is an 'AOT compiler' (ahead-of-time), and how does it differ from a JIT compiler?
options: AOT compiles before runtime (producing standalone binaries); JIT compiles at runtime during execution
AOT and JIT are identical approaches with different historical names
JIT compilation is always faster than AOT because it has profiling information
AOT is only possible for statically typed languages
questions.explanation: AOT (traditional C/C++ compilers) produces an executable before the program runs. JIT compiles during execution, using runtime profiling. AOT is predictable (no startup delay); JIT adapts to actual behavior. Modern VMs (Java) combine both: AOT for quick start, JIT for hot code.

questions.q: What is a 'context switch' in virtual machines, and why is it costly?
options: Switching between different thread/fiber contexts involves saving and restoring CPU registers and memory state, causing overhead
Context switching is only relevant to bytecode interpreters, not CPUs
Context switches never happen in single-threaded programs
Context switching has no performance impact on modern systems
questions.explanation: Context switches occur when the OS or VM switches execution from one thread to another. Saving/restoring registers, cache invalidation, and TLB misses incur overhead. Lightweight contexts (fibers, green threads) reduce this cost but can't use true parallelism. VM design (thread pools, workstealing) mitigates it.

questions.q: What is a 'trace' in the context of tracing JIT compilation?
options: A trace is a recorded path of execution through code; tracing JIT compiles frequently-taken traces to machine code
A trace is a debug log of program execution printed to standard output
Traces are only relevant to interpreted languages and have no role in compilation
Trace-based JIT is slower than method-based JIT because it compiles less code
questions.explanation: Tracing JIT (used in PyPy, LuaJIT) records actual execution paths and compiles them. Traces may skip rarely-taken branches, enabling aggressive optimizations. Method-based JIT (Java) compiles entire functions. Tracing excels at data-dependent loops; method-based is simpler. Trade-off: smaller/faster traces vs. larger/compilable methods.

questions.q: What role does 'escape analysis' play in JIT compilation optimization?
options: Escape analysis determines if objects remain local to a function; if not, they 'escape' and must be heap-allocated; if they don't escape, they can be stack-allocated or eliminated
Escape analysis is a debugging technique to find memory leaks
Escape analysis only applies to garbage-collected languages, not manual memory management
Escape analysis provides no performance benefit and is unnecessary
questions.explanation: When an object allocated in a function never escapes (not returned, not stored globally), the JIT can stack-allocate or even eliminate it entirely via scalar replacement. If it escapes, it must be heap-allocated. This optimization significantly reduces GC pressure and improves cache locality in JIT-compiled code.
